commentaries.author: Augustine of Hippo
commentaries.content: 2. "Confess unto the Lord that He is sweet, because for aye in His mercy" (ver. 1). This confess ye that He is sweet: if ye have tasted, confess. But he cannot confess, who hath not chosen to taste, for whence shall he say that that is sweet, which he knoweth not. But ye if ye have tasted how sweet the Lord is, "Confess ye to the Lord that He is sweet." If ye have tasted with eagerness, break forth with confession. "For aye is His mercy," that is, for ever. For here "for aye," is so put, since also in some other places of Scripture, for aye, that is, what in Greek is called eij aiwna, is understood for ever. For His mercy is not for a time, so as not to be for ever, since for this purpose His present mercy is over men, that they may live with the Angels for ever.

commentaries.author: Augustine of Hippo
commentaries.content: This Psalm commendeth unto us the mercies of God, proved in ourselves, and is therefore the sweeter to the experienced. And it is a wonder if it can be pleasing to any one, except to him who has learned in his own case, what he hears in this Psalm. Yet was it written not for any one or two, but for the people of God, and set forth that it might know itself therein as in a mirror. Its title needeth not now to be treated, for it is Halleluia, and again Halleluia. Which we have a custom of singing at a certain time in our solemnities, after an old tradition of the Church: nor is it without a sacred meaning that we sing it on particular days. Halleluia we sing indeed on certain days, but every day we think it. For if in this word is signified the praise of God, though not in the mouth of the flesh, yet surely in the mouth of the heart. "His praise shall ever be in my mouth. " But that the title hath Halleluia not once only but twice, is not peculiar to this Psalm, but the former also hath it so. And as far as appears from its text, that was sung of the people of Israel, but this is sung of the universal Church of God, spread through the whole world. Perchance, it not unfitly hath Halleluia twice, because we cry, Abba, Father. Since Abba is nothing else but Father, yet not without meaning the Apostle said, "in whom we cry, Abba, Father;" but because one wall indeed coming to the Corner Stone crieth Abba, but the other, from the other side crieth Father; viz. , in that Corner Stone, "who is our Peace, who hath made bothone.

commentaries.author: George Leo Haydock
commentaries.content: Augustine repeats this word. But it occurs in the Hebrew, at the end of the preceding psalm, of which this is a continuation, shewing how God pardoned his people, and delivered them from captivity, (Calmet) and mankind from sin. (Haydock) (Fathers) (Berthier) Glory. Literally, "confess "your sins, thay you may praise God, (Psalm cv. ; Haydock) and adore his mercy and providence. (Worthington) Ver. The Jews from Babylon, and all who were redeemed by Christ. (Calmet) He was promised immediately after the fall. Hebrew miyam, for which we should read imim "the right "denoting the south. (Calmet) But the sea, or ocean, is properly used in the same sense. (Berthier) The Ammonites, Philistines, Syrians, and Idumeans, from these four quarters, often reduced the Israelites to servitude, under the judges. (Houbigant) From all parts, the Jews of the ten tribes returned in the reigns of Darius and Alexander. (Calmet, Diss. ) But the texts which are applied to this event speak rather of the conversion of the Gentiles, which the prophet has here in view. (Berthier) None are excluded from the benefits of this redemption, but by their own fault. So were the Jews distressed at Babylon, (Calmet) as many are forced to wander in the world, and all are involved in sin. As both Jews and Gentiles were for the word of God, (Amos viii. ) when Christ appeared. (Haydock) Their wants cried aloud, (St. Augustine) though they sought him not, Isaias lxv. (Berthier) Those who call upon God are relieved by him, in the manner which is most for their welfare. We are unworthy to open our mouths. This chorus is repeated, (ver. ) by the people, after the Levites had sung the intermediate sentences. (Calmet) The ver. , are of the same nature, and refer to different sorts of calamities. (Berthier) All the benefits of God, proceeding from his mercy, and not from man's deserts, praise him. This second allegory represents the condition of the captives, and of mankind, before Christ's coming. (Calmet) The former enjoyed some liberty, Jeremias xxix. But the latter were most miserable, (Matthew ix. , and Luke iv. ) and of these the words are most naturally explained. (Berthier) Ver. Exasperated. Hebrew, "changed, or frustrated. "This is the source of all misery, (Calmet) as calamities are commonly inflicted on account of sin. There were slaves of various descriptions: some were confined to hard labour, and chained down at night. (Calmet) Ver. Of their passions. Augustine, Conf. (Haydock) Ver. Making them alter their conduct. Hebrew seems less correct: "they are foolish on account of their way, and they "Illness is sent to punish sin, Job xxxiii. , Matthew ix. , and John v. Jesus Christ, according to the prophetical sense, adopted by the Fathers, (St. Athanasius, or. ) though it literally implies, that at God's command the sick were healed, Matthew viii. This allegory again represents the state of captivity. Instead of victims, Psalm xxvi. Down to the sea Captivity is here compared to a tempest. (Calmet) The apostles carry the tidings of salvation to all places. Persecutions are raised; but God grants peace to his Church under Constantine. (Eusebius) Ver. The fury of tempests. Various nations, and the treasures of the deep. (Menochius) Ver. So Virgil says: Tollimur in cælum curvato gurgiteiidem Subducta ad Manes imos descend Imus unda. They knew not how to proceed. Ambiguis ars stupet ipsa malis. Hebrew, "silence, or calm. Even to Jerusalem. We must thank God for having enabled us to repent, ver. God caused his people to pass through the Red Sea, and the Jordan, to possess the fruitful country of Chanaan, part of which had been cursed for the crimes of the Sodomites. (Haydock) He has punished Babylon, (Jeremias xxv. ) made a straight road for his people, (Isaias xxxv. ) and enabled them to cultivate their country again, ver. (Calmet) The synagogue has been abandoned, and the Church chosen. (Berthier) The power of God is displayed, who caused the land of promise to be more fertile for his people, which is now very barren. Or "saltness. "(Haydock) He alludes to the environs of Sodom, Genesis xiii. A plentiful crop, facient fruges germina. Jerome) (Calmet) Abundance of fruit shall grow in some places, while others are punished on account of sin. Jechonias was humbled, (Haydock) and exalted, with Daniel, 4 Kings xxv. Others explain it of Nabuchodonosor and Baltasar, who were reduced to the state of ignominy and death. (Calmet) The Jews experienced the greatest miseries at the last siege of Jerusalem. The Gentiles took the place of the obstinate Jews. Augustine) Ver. The psalmist saw Babylon before its ruin, (Calmet) at least in spirit. God's judgments strengthen virtue, and repress the wicked. (Haydock) The propagation of the gospel put to silence the oracles of the pagans. This is the part of wisdom, and deserves our serious consideration, (Haydock) as Osee (xiv. ) and our Saviour admonish, Matthew xi. , and Mark iv.

methodology.step_3: Use Firecrawl to get full content from 'Go to Commentary' links
methodology.step_4: Fallback to BeautifulSoup for accordion content if needed
advantages: Records verses even without commentaries for complete tracking
